message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this proposed lever for improving a RACE score. Default to refuted=true when uncertain.

LEVER: Padding cut to fund the synthesis budget
TARGETS: instruction_following (0.25) / Consistent Focus on Restructuring Impact w=0.20; readability P1 'appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy' w=0.15 (weight 0.25)
CHANGE: Deletion pass over the composed body: (1) metadata-as-content sentences ('published in the Strategic Management Journal', 'received 239 Scopus citations', ALMATO bot counts [36], '525 papers... only 20 papers' [103]); (2) off-topic digressions (AI-lit-review rejection [91][92], Toptal ad content [55], call-center market sizing [99], resume/hiring-process surveys [54] unless reframed as labor-market matching); (3) same-fact-two-sources duplicates (the ChatGPT '2x as likely to report using it' sentence appears verbatim twice, lines 19 and 27, cited to [41] and [61] — catch via fuzzy-match dedup across DIFFERENT citation keys, which is why every existing dedup missed it). Spend the freed ~1,500-2,000 words on the synthesis paragraphs and sector section — this is what keeps total length at reference parity.
CLAIMED GAIN: +0.005 to +0.010 direct; primarily an ENABLER — without it, levers 3-4 either blow past 9k words or cannibalize coverage
CLAIMED FAILURE MODE: Over-aggressive deletion cuts facts the judge counts for breadth, re-opening the comprehensiveness gap; fuzzy dedup at low thresholds merges genuinely distinct findings (e.g. the two different 5.4%-adoption stats in [94] and [95] are the same fact, but the 15% [42] vs 13.8% [71] productivity numbers are different specifications of the same study — keep one, say why). Human-review the deletion list once before automating.

ATTACK IT THROUGH THIS LENS:
NOISE: is the claimed gain distinguishable from the measured +/-0.016 noise floor on a single task? If not, it is unfalsifiable as specified.

Context: our report /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md scored 0.4313. Reference /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md = 0.5000 by definition. SOTA = 0.5265.
Read the files if you need to check a claim. Be adversarial, concrete, and brief.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
19:The study also documents a 1.2 percentage point decrease in the probability of receiving any employment in a given month—approximately a 10% drop relative to baseline—and a 4.7% reduction in the number of jobs conditional on being employed.[38] By contrast, aggregate labor-market indicators through 2024–2025 show limited economywide disruption; one analysis reports that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 but finds only small positive wage effects and no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations.[39] A prominent Harvard University study tracking 62 million workers across 285,000 U.S. firms finds that junior positions have been shrinking at companies integrating AI since 2023, with researchers warning that AI is eroding the bottom rungs of career ladders.[40] This finding is consistent with online labor market evidence that entry-level and less-experienced workers in highly exposed occupations bear the brunt of early displacement effects.[38][39] In quantitative terms, treated participants completed the post-treatment task 11 minutes faster—a 0.75 standard deviation reduction—while evaluator grades increased by 0.45 standard deviations.[41] The experiment also documents a reduction in productivity inequality: the correlation between first-task and second-task grades fell from 0.41 in the control group to 0.14 in the treatment group, driven by larger gains for participants who scored lower initially.[41] Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41] In a large-scale field study, the staggered introduction of a generative AI conversational assistant to 5,172 customer-support agents increased productivity, measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average.[42] Field experiments in online retail similarly document treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% in sales, with smaller and newer sellers as well as less experienced consumers exhibiting disproportionately larger gains.[43] A comprehensive review of the empirical literature finds task-completion time reductions of 15% to more than 50% across writing, customer support, software development, accounting, law, and translation.[39] In software development specifically, GitHub Copilot users complete coding tasks 55.8% faster in controlled settings, with larger gains among less experienced developers.[39] A separate study of nearly 5,000 developers across three field experiments finds a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion, again driven by higher adoption and disproportionately larger gains for junior developers.[39] In a randomized trial with 300 professional translators, a tenfold increase in training compute reduced completion time by 12.3%, improved quality by 0.18 standard deviations, and raised earnings per minute by 16.1%, with lower-skilled translators experiencing gains roughly four times larger than higher-skilled peers.[39] These productivity gains exhibit a consistent pattern of skill compression, whereby less-experienced and lower-skilled workers benefit disproportionately from AI assistance.[39][41][42] Exposure measures across multiple studies converge toward high-wage jobs, with correlations between AI exposure and wages ranging from 0.27 to 0.54.[44] A separate review finds that automating firms show 2% to 10% higher employment and positive elasticities between 0.2% and 2%, suggesting complementarity at the firm level even where aggregate displacement is detectable.[45] The analysis finds that a 1% increase in freelancers' earnings is associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in the number of jobs and a 1.7% reduction in monthly income for text-based services.[38]
27:A joint analysis by the Brookings Institution and the Stanford Digital Economy Lab finds that over 30 percent of U.S. workers could see at least half of their job tasks affected by generative AI, with exposure concentrated in white-collar and entry-level occupations rather than in traditional manufacturing or manual labor.[58] One comprehensive displacement analysis projects that 85 million jobs will be displaced by 2025, while 97 million new roles will simultaneously emerge, representing a net positive job creation of 12 million positions globally.[59] However, the gendered dimension of this restructuring is significant: geographic analysis indicates that 58.87 million women in the U.S. workforce occupy positions highly exposed to AI automation compared to 48.62 million men, highlighting substantial gender disparities in vulnerability.[59] Customer service representatives face the highest immediate risk with an 80 percent automation rate by 2025, followed by data entry clerks with 7.5 million jobs eliminated by 2027 and retail cashiers facing 65 percent automation risk by 2025.[59] Regional variations show North America leading automation adoption at 70 percent by 2025.[59] The OECD Employment Outlook 2023 finds that the share of the employed population with AI skills is small—at most 0.3 percent of those employed in OECD countries on average—but growing rapidly.[60] Researchers find that a one standard deviation increase in exposure to AI is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, largely driven by occupations that require a high level of familiarity with software.[60] However, men—particularly in finance—were more likely than women to expect a wage increase and less likely to expect a wage decrease due to AI, indicating that AI may put further pressure on currently existing wage inequalities.[60] The OECD case studies of AI implementation find that, so far, AI has led to little wage change.[60] Beyond wages, AI affects job quality through changes in task content and the working environment, with the potential to reduce tedious or dangerous tasks but also to leave workers with a higher-paced work environment.[60] Workers exposed to ChatGPT during the experiment were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after the experiment.[61] The international digital divide compounds these distributional effects, as cross-country differences in occupational structure and digital infrastructure create an asymmetry between advanced and developing economies.[62] In Mali, just 12 percent of today's jobs are exposed to GenAI, but only 10 percent of such workers have internet access, meaning just 1.2 percent of workers in Mali hold jobs that are both exposed to GenAI and can make practical use of it.[62] In contrast, about one out of three workers in Switzerland and Singapore have jobs exposed to GenAI, and all of them have access to the internet.[62] Among all countries in the sample, there are 441.8 million jobs that fall within exposure categories that could leverage GenAI for job augmentation, but 66.9 million, or 15.1 percent, of those jobs do not have internet access.[62] Workers in positions vulnerable to automation typically maintain sufficient internet connectivity to experience displacement effects even in low-income settings, while those who could benefit from GenAI augmentation face substantial digital infrastructure gaps that may prevent them from realizing productivity gains.[62] This finding suggests that developing countries may experience the disruptive effects of GenAI faster than its productivity benefits.[62] There are age disparities in exposure as well, particularly in upper-middle-income countries, where about 10.4 percent of workers aged 16 to 35 face automation risk, while only 4.3 percent of workers aged 56 to 65 are in that position.[62] Among college graduates in low-income countries, 13.2 percent face GenAI automation exposure, and 72 percent of these workers already have internet access, implying that initial displacement effects could concentrate among the narrow segment of skilled and digitally-connected workers.[62] Generative AI also affects labor market matching efficiency, with a field experiment finding that AI-assisted job postings made employers 19 percent more likely to post a job and saved them 44 percent of writing time, but there was no discernible increase in matches formed.[63]
--- lines 19,27

content.content:
154
15:Early foundational work by Frey and Osborne estimated the probability of computerisation for 702 detailed occupations using a Gaussian process classifier, establishing a task-based exposure methodology that became dominant in the field of AI labor market research.[24][25] Building on this paradigm, Felten, Raj, and Seamans developed a novel dataset measuring occupational, industry, and geographic exposure to artificial intelligence, published in the Strategic Management Journal.[26] Their approach, unlike the original Frey and Osborne framework, allowed temporal updating but still did not distinguish between AI types such as generative AI versus symbolic AI.[25] That study estimated that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces and general training.[27] When accounting for current and likely future software developments that complement LLM capabilities, this share jumps to just over 46% of jobs.[27] A separate text-mining analysis of the International Standard Occupation Classification found that 32.8% of occupations could be fully impacted by ChatGPT, while 36.5% might experience a partial impact and 30.7% are likely to remain unaffected.[28] For the 32.8% of occupations facing a full impact, the effects may be particularly pronounced and disruptive, whereas the 30.7% with no impact will likely continue performing their jobs as before.[28] That study found that the cognitive abilities most exposed to the recent surge of AI research are ideas-related, including attention and search, comprehension and expression, and logical reasoning.[29] This generative AI era finding calls into question pre-GenAI assessments of which workers and tasks are most exposed, as earlier automation primarily targeted routine tasks while GenAI can execute complex, nonstandardized functions traditionally reliant on human judgment.[29][30] The O*NET framework used to classify occupational tasks defines core tasks as those with a relevance rating greater than 67 percent and a mean importance rating of more than 3.0.[30] Global business investment in AI increased 13-fold from US$14.57 billion in 2013 to US$189 billion in 2023, with 35% of businesses already using AI in their operations and more than 50% intending to implement AI technologies.[31] However, more recent data show that the share of STEM jobs grew from 6.5 percent in 2010 to nearly 10 percent in 2024, representing an almost 50 percent increase.[32] Between 2013 and 2023, the share of retail sales jobs dropped from 7.5 to 5.7 percent of the job market, a reduction of 25 percent, with the e-commerce sector identified as an early adopter of predictive AI.[32] By 2023, the International Federation of Robotics reported that average robot density in manufacturing worldwide reached a new high of 141 robots per 10,000 employees.[33] The same study found that job replacement risk is highest in manufacturing and retail, closely followed by the transportation and materials sector, while healthcare and education will proportionately gain more than they lose from automation.[33] Jobs for AI and Machine Learning specialists rose at a compounding rate of 32% per year between 2016 and 2023, illustrating the dual nature of AI as both a destroyer and creator of employment.[33] A critical survey of the empirical literature on generative AI's labor market impact found that no consistent evidence has yet emerged of systemic labor market disruption attributable to generative AI.[25] One study using a difference-in-differences design over 1.2 million contracts on Upwork found that in the five months after ChatGPT's release, treated occupations experienced up to a 20% reduction in earnings.[25] Recent meta-analytic studies confirmed the absence of statistically significant average effects of AI adoption on employment or wages and detected no robust signs of publication bias.[25] Displacement occurs in certain occupations, especially within digital freelance markets, while other sectors exhibit productivity gains or wage premia, reflecting the still-emergent diffusion of AI and its uneven adoption across industries and countries.[25] A risk management perspective emphasizes that the extensive acceptance of AI is likely to result in employment displacement, as many previously human-performed tasks become automatable, necessitating investment in education to facilitate workforce transitions into new roles.[34] In the pharmaceutical sector, the adoption of generative AI could lead to an annual increase in value added by 2.6 to 4.5 percent, primarily via assistance with research and development of new drugs.[35] By 2018, the company had expanded to over 1,500 Frontend Assistants supporting 4 million monthly transactions, performing tasks such as checking connections, entering orders, generating invoices, and processing cancellations.[36] A global AI conceptual framework developed within the International Futures platform produces dynamic forecasts for 186 countries, modeling interconnected aspects of human development including economics, demographics, and energy, providing a macro-level system for understanding long-range AI impacts.[37] Because the associated information processing and problem-solving tasks are the most transversal across occupations, the analysis documented an exponential increase in AI exposure across all occupational categories, even though comparatively high-skilled occupations are more exposed than elementary occupations.[29] A systematic literature review covering publications from 2015 to July 2025 identified research on AI and job displacement with major contributions from China, the US, and Germany.[31] An organizational study of automation found divergent perspectives between management and employees: business leaders rated workflow simplification positively with a mean of 4.19, while employees rated it 4.03, and work-life balance was rated much lower by employees at 2.26 compared to 2.99 by business leaders.[36] ALMATO deployed around 400 software bots by 2016, automating more than 40 processes across 30 different applications and handling roughly 320,000 business transactions each month.[36] Researchers have employed varied methodologies, including Gaussian process classifiers, text-mining approaches, benchmark-linked cognitive ability mapping, and integrated assessment platforms, to assess occupational exposure to computerisation and AI technologies.[24][28][29][37]
43:By contrast, a more cautious task-based framework by Acemoglu et al. estimates that AI's contribution to total factor productivity gains may not exceed 0.7 percentage points over ten years even under full automation of exposed tasks, with GDP impact remaining modest at approximately 1.1%.[93] IMF simulations by Cerutti et al. illustrate two contrasting trajectories: a rapid adoption scenario in which global TFP grows by 2.4% over ten years and global GDP increases by 4%, versus a slower scenario with TFP growth of only 1.8% and GDP growth limited to approximately 1.3%.[93] Census Bureau study reported that only 5.4% of firms were using AI technologies in spring 2024, up from 3.7% the year prior.[94] A separate analysis by Bonney et al. documents that as of February 2024, only 5.4% of U.S. firms reported using AI, with adoption in the leading sector (Information) still below 20%.[95] Goldman Sachs similarly finds that only 9.3% of companies in a recent U.S. survey reported having used generative AI in production during the prior two weeks, concluding that low adoption is limiting overall labor-market impacts.[96] Average time savings from AI chatbots in that study amounted to 2.8% of work hours, which contrasts with productivity gains exceeding 15% documented by randomized controlled trials in the same occupations.[95] A study published in Science found that generative AI can boost output by as much as 40% in text-based tasks, but novices who accept machine suggestions uncritically perform worse than those who reason through problems themselves.[97] Research on job transformation develops a general-equilibrium model in which moderate AI exposure benefits workers on average but high exposure harms them, with the return to social skills rising and the return to analytical skills falling.[98] Gmyrek et al. estimate that only 3.3% of jobs will be exposed to full automation, a figure that rises to 7% in the analysis by Chui et al., reinforcing the consensus that AI is transforming jobs more than eliminating them.[93] Goldman Sachs Research estimates that if current AI use cases were expanded across the economy, just 2.5% of U.S. employment would be at risk of displacement.[96] In the customer service domain, only 25% of call centers have successfully integrated AI automation into daily operations as of 2026, while 91% of customer service leaders report feeling pressure to implement AI.[99] The global AI customer service market is projected at $15.12 billion in 2026, growing at a 25.8% compound annual rate toward $47.82 billion by 2030.[99] Historical data on industrial robot density illustrate stark sectoral variation in automation: in 2014, there were an estimated 0.02 industrial robots per 1,000 workers in construction compared with 117.72 per 1,000 in automotive production.[100] The WEF reports that global average robot density reached 162 units per 10,000 employees in 2023, double the number measured seven years prior, with 80% of installations concentrated in China, Japan, and the United States.[101] AI-driven recruitment tools have also raised fairness concerns, with scholars noting that AI applications in hiring can amplify and introduce new forms of intersectional discrimination given datasets structured along axes of social inequality.[102] The analysis of Industry 4.0 adoption in supply chain operations found 525 papers related to Industry 4.0 broadly, but only 20 papers specifically addressed supply chain dimensions, highlighting a significant research gap.[103] The Future of Jobs Report finds that 86% of respondents expect AI and information processing technologies to transform their business by 2030, positioning AI as the technology with the single largest anticipated impact.[101] A large-scale study of AI chatbot adoption across 11 exposed occupations in Denmark, linked to administrative data, estimates precise null effects on earnings and recorded hours at both the individual and workplace levels, with confidence intervals ruling out effects above 1%.[95] Nearly all respondents reported that AI chatbots have had no impact on their earnings.[95] That same study observed a 3.7-minute decrease in average handle time, representing an 8.5% decline from a baseline of 43 minutes.[104] Morgan reports that the unemployment rate among college graduates reached 5.8% in March, the highest in more than four years.[105]

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
t=open('report.md').read()
body=t.split('## References')[0] if '## References' in t else t
# split sentences
sents=re.split(r'(?<=[.\]])\s+(?=[A-Z])',body)
pats=['Strategic Management Journal','Scopus','ALMATO','Toptal','525 papers','call center','customer service market','resume','hiring process','1,500 Frontend']
hits=[s for s in sents if any(p.lower() in s.lower() for p in pats)]
print("candidate sents:",len(hits),"words:",sum(len(s.split()) for s in hits))
print("total body words:",len(body.split()))
for h in hits[:12]: print("-",h[:120])
EOF

print("ref body words:",len(b.split()),"total:",len(t.split()))

cd /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re,itertools
from difflib import SequenceMatcher
t=open('report.md').read().split('## References')[0]
s=[x for x in re.split(r'(?<=\])\s+(?=[A-Z])',t) if len(x.split())>12]
pairs=[]
for a,b in itertools.combinations(range(len(s)),2):
    x=re.sub(r'\[\d+\]','',s[a]); y=re.sub(r'\[\d+\]','',s[b])
    r=SequenceMatcher(None,x,y).ratio()
    if r>0.62: pairs.append((round(r,2),len(y.split()),s[a][:70],s[b][:70]))
pairs.sort(reverse=True)
print("near-dup pairs >0.62:",len(pairs),"recoverable words:",sum(p[1] for p in pairs))
for p in pairs[:8]: print(p)
EOF

(0.7, 25, 'One analysis finds that the evidence on how AI is affecting the labor ', 'The evidence on how AI is affecting the labor market today is inconclu')

content.text:
Verified against the artifacts. Refuting.

**1. The claimed gain is inside the noise floor by construction.** `/home/polaris/polaris_project/OVERNIGHT_PROGRESS.md:305-307` records the measured RACE judge variance on this exact harness: the champion artifact re-scored 0.4291 vs. an original 0.4447 — σ ≈ 0.016 on an *unchanged* document. The lever claims +0.005 to +0.010, i.e. 30–60% of one noise SD. To detect a +0.0075 effect against σ=0.016 at 80% power you need on the order of 35+ paired scored runs per arm. Nobody is going to run that. As specified, the direct claim is unfalsifiable — it can be neither confirmed nor refuted by any measurement this pipeline actually performs.

**2. The "enabler" framing makes it permanently unattributable.** The lever's own fallback ("primarily an ENABLER — without it, levers 3-4 blow past 9k") concedes the direct effect is not the point. But that converts it into a claim that can only ever be measured jointly with levers 3-4, and any joint measurement confounds it. There is no experiment that isolates it. That is the definition of an unfalsifiable lever.

**3. The word budget is fabricated by ~3-4x.** I measured it:
- Body of `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md` = **7,742 words** (9,194 incl. references).
- Every sentence matching the lever's *named* padding patterns (Strategic Management Journal, Scopus, ALMATO/Frontend Assistants, Toptal, 525-papers, call-center market sizing, resume/hiring surveys) = **11 sentences, 488 words total**. And 4 of those 11 (~180w) are the hiring/matching/cover-letter material the lever itself admits is legitimately on-topic under "labor-market matching."
- Cross-citation-key fuzzy dedup, run at a *generous* 0.62 threshold: **exactly 2 near-duplicate pairs, 60 recoverable words.** Full pool.

Total defensible deletion: **~550 words, ~7% of body** — not 1,500–2,000. Getting to the claimed budget requires deleting ~1,000+ words of *unnamed* content, which is precisely the stated failure mode (breadth loss) and is the part the lever does not specify.

**4. The premise that a length budget is even binding is wrong.** `RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md` is **9,029 words of body with no reference section**. Our body is 7,742 — we are already **1,290 words UNDER** reference body length. The "9k ceiling" that levers 3-4 supposedly blow past isn't a ceiling; ~9k of *body* IS parity. So there is no budget to free.

**5. Best-case rubric arithmetic still lands in the noise.** readability P1 is w=0.15 within a 0.25 dimension = 0.0375 of total. A *full point* gain on a 5-pt scale = +0.0075 — the entire claimed range — while the same pass risks comprehensiveness. Deleting 11 sentences from a 9k-word doc will not move a coarse judge a full point.

**Salvage:** the duplicate is real (line 19 `[41]` "2x as likely to report using it… 1.6x" vs line 27 `[61]` "2 times as likely… 1.6 times" — paraphrased, not verbatim as the lever claims). Cross-key fuzzy dedup is cheap, deterministic, and zero-risk — ship it as hygiene folded into a scored lever. Do not budget words against it and do not expect an attributable delta.

input.lever: Padding cut to fund the synthesis budget
input.reasoning:
NOISE: The claimed +0.005-0.010 is 30-60% of the measured judge noise SD. OVERNIGHT_PROGRESS.md:305-307 documents an UNCHANGED artifact re-scoring 0.4447 -> 0.4291 (sigma ~= 0.016) on this exact harness. Detecting +0.0075 against sigma=0.016 at 80% power needs ~35+ paired scored runs per arm; the pipeline runs 1. The direct claim is therefore unfalsifiable as specified. The lever's own fallback ("primarily an ENABLER") concedes this and makes it worse: an enabler can only be measured jointly with levers 3-4, so it is unattributable by construction -- there exists no experiment that isolates it.

BUDGET IS FABRICATED (measured, not asserted): report.md body = 7,742 words (9,194 incl. refs). Every sentence matching the lever's NAMED padding patterns (Strategic Management Journal, Scopus, ALMATO/Frontend Assistants, Toptal, 525-papers, call-center market sizing, resume/hiring surveys) totals 11 sentences / 488 words -- and 4 of those 11 (~180w) are the hiring/matching/cover-letter material the lever itself concedes is on-topic under "labor-market matching." Cross-citation-key fuzzy dedup run at a generous 0.62 threshold finds EXACTLY 2 near-duplicate pairs = 60 recoverable words, total pool. So the defensible deletion is ~550 words (~7% of body), not the claimed 1,500-2,000. Reaching the claimed budget requires cutting ~1,000+ words of unnamed content -- exactly the stated failure mode (breadth loss), and the unspecified part.

PREMISE ERROR ON LENGTH: RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md is 9,029 words of BODY with no reference section. Our body is 7,742 -- we are already 1,290 words UNDER reference body length. The "9k ceiling" levers 3-4 would supposedly blow past is not a ceiling; ~9k of body IS parity. There is no length budget to free, so the enabler rationale collapses.

CEILING ARITHMETIC: readability P1 is w=0.15 inside a 0.25 dimension = 0.0375 of total score. Even a full 1-point gain on a 5-pt criterion yields +0.0075 -- the entire claimed range -- and deleting 11 sentences from a 9k-word document will not move a coarse judge a full point, while the same pass risks comprehensiveness points. Best case is inside noise and has an offsetting downside.
input.salvage: The duplicate is REAL and worth fixing: line 19 "[41] ... 2x as likely to report using it ... 1.6x" vs line 27 "[61] ... 2 times as likely ... 1.6 times" (paraphrased, not verbatim as the lever states -- which is why exact-match dedup missed it). Cross-citation-key fuzzy dedup is cheap, deterministic, and zero-risk: ship it as hygiene folded into a lever that IS scored. Same for relocating the ~300w of genuine metadata-as-content (Scopus counts, ALMATO bot counts, 525-papers, Toptal). But: do NOT budget 1,500-2,000 words against it, do NOT gate levers 3-4 on it, and do NOT expect an attributable score delta -- the entire real pool is ~550 words / 7% of body.
